title: Who is Your God?
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Who is Your God_ - Pastor Tito Lyro - 1 Samuel 2_1-11.txt
outline: Introduction: Hannah's song of praise after Samuel's birth
My heart rejoices in the LORD — personal praise
There is none holy as the LORD, no rock like our God
God brings down the proud and lifts up the humble
The LORD kills and makes alive, brings down and raises up
He raises the poor from the dust and the needy from the ash heap
Conclusion: Who is your God? — the sovereign LORD of Hannah's song
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from 1 Samuel 2:1-11, Hannah's magnificent song of praise after the birth of Samuel. The sermon explores Hannah's declaration of God's unique holiness and sovereignty — how He reverses the fortunes of the proud and humble, the rich and poor — and asks: who is your God?
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: 1 Samuel 2:1–11

The question I am attempting to answer this morning — and a question I want you to ask yourself — is: who is your God? Listen to Hannah's song as I read her prayer and ask yourself: who is my God?

> *"And Hannah prayed and said: 'My heart rejoices in the Lord; my horn is exalted in the Lord. I smile at my enemies, because I rejoice in Your salvation. No one is holy like the Lord, for there is none besides You, nor is there any rock like our God. Talk no more so very proudly; let no arrogance come from your mouth, for the Lord is the God of knowledge; and by Him actions are weighed. The bows of the mighty men are broken, and those who stumbled are girded with strength. Those who were full have hired themselves out for bread, and the hungry have ceased to hunger. Even the barren has borne seven, and she who has many children has become feeble. The Lord kills and makes alive; He brings down to the grave and brings up. The Lord makes poor and makes rich; He brings low and lifts up. He raises the poor from the dust and lifts the beggar from the ash heap, to set them among princes and make them inherit the throne of glory. For the pillars of the earth are the Lord's, and He has set the world upon them. He will guard the feet of His saints, but the wicked shall be silent in darkness. For by strength no man shall prevail. The adversaries of the Lord shall be broken in pieces; from heaven He will thunder against them. The Lord will judge the ends of the earth. He will give strength to His king, and exalt the horn of His anointed.' Then Elkanah went to his house at Ramah. But the child ministered to the Lord before Eli the priest."* (1 Samuel 2:1–11)

## Introduction: The Desire to Know God

The greatest desire of the Apostle Paul was to know Jesus better. In Philippians 3:7–11, he says:

> *"But what things were gain to me, these I have counted loss for Christ. Yet indeed I also count all things loss for the excellence of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord."*

Paul describes this desire — the desire to know God, to know Jesus — as the normative experience of a Christian. If you have no desire to know who God truly is, you need to look at your heart and ask: do I truly trust in Jesus as my Savior and my Lord? The desire to know God is a necessary consequence of having been redeemed by the Spirit of God and come to faith in the Lord Jesus Christ.

Psalm 34:8 invites us to *taste* God — the same word used for taking a bite of fruit. Try him out. See if what he says is not true.

As we read Hannah's song, we are struck by how well she knew her God. She had tasted of him deeply and had seen that he was indeed good. Her song is a response to knowing who God is and how he works, and it exhorts us today to know our God — to taste him, so that we may see that he is good.

## What Led Hannah to This Prayer?

Hannah found herself in a difficult family situation. Elkanah had two wives: Peninnah was having many children while Hannah was barren. Elkanah tried to comfort her — but he didn't really grasp her situation. "Hannah, you have me — why do you need a child?" That wasn't quite the right response.

In faith, Hannah took her issue to the Lord, knowing that God is both the God of the fruitful woman and the God of the barren woman (verse 5). She came and prayed in the Tabernacle at Shiloh. She was so absorbed in prayer that Eli thought she was drunk. She explained: "No, I'm praying to the Lord" — asking for a son whom she would raise for the Lord. It was not just a selfish desire; she wanted to give that child back to God.

When she rose from prayer, she left rejoicing — even before there was any answer — because she knew that whatever God's answer would be, it would come from the hand of a good God. The Lord gave her Samuel, and now in chapter 2, she is returning Samuel to the Lord as she had promised.

Hannah's prayer in verse 1 is labeled as a prayer, but it is written as a song — Hebrew poetry, similar to other hymns in the Old Testament. It is one of a few songs in the Bible sung by women. And interestingly, at the end of the book of 2 Samuel (chapter 22), David's great song of praise parallels Hannah's song — the two books function as a literary unit, with these songs as inclusions framing the whole.

## Four Things Hannah Knew About God

Hannah bases her joy and thankfulness on four things she knew about the Lord, seen in verses 2–10.

### 1. She Knew God's Nature (vv. 2–3)

> *"No one is holy like the Lord, for there is none besides You, nor is there any rock like our God. Talk no more so very proudly..."*

**God is holy.** For the unbeliever, God's holiness is terrifying — it means you will be judged for your sins. For the believer, it is an encouragement: in his holiness, he has given us his Son so that we might be holy as well.

Do you realize what happens the moment you believe? Two things:

First, God declares you innocent of every sin you have ever committed or ever will commit — *justification*. The guilt of all your sins is laid upon Christ on the cross.

Second, everything Jesus did in his perfect life is counted as yours — what is sometimes called *definitive sanctification*. Before God, positionally, God sees you not merely as "not guilty" (neutral) but as positively righteous in the same way Christ is righteous. That is what your God has done for you because he is holy.

**God is one.** There are no other gods besides the God of the Bible. Our idols are nothing but reflections of ourselves — we are very good at worshiping ourselves. But there is no other God who will thwart God's plans. There is no balance of forces between God and Satan, no Star Wars yin and yang. God will do what he wants to do. If he could not do whatever he wants, he would not be God.

**God is strong.** Hannah calls him a *rock* — a strong foundation, protection, immovable. We can build our lives upon him.

> *"What then shall we say to these things? If God is for us, who can be against us?"* (Romans 8:31)

Believer, who can be against you if God is for you? If someone kills you, what happens? You see Jesus. That is the worst that can happen — and it is the blessed hope.

### 2. She Knew God's Providential Government of All Things (vv. 4–8)

Look at the contrasting pairs in verses 4–8: the mighty are broken while the weak are strengthened; the full hire themselves out for bread while the hungry cease to hunger; the barren bears seven while the mother of many becomes feeble. God raises the poor from the dust and lifts the beggar from the ash heap to set them among princes.

Hannah found great comfort in the absolute sovereignty of God. Nothing happens outside of God's control — not a breath we take, not a hair that falls from our heads.

> *"For of Him and through Him and to Him are all things, to whom be glory forever."* (Romans 11:36)

Our Westminster Confession says: "God from all eternity did, by the most wise and holy counsel of His own will, freely and unchangeably ordain whatsoever comes to pass; yet so as thereby neither is God the author of sin, nor is violence offered to the will of the creatures..."

He is sovereign over angels — good and evil. Sovereign over nature. Sovereign over so-called chance events. Sovereign over nations. Sovereign over the free and even the sinful actions of his creatures. The doctrine of God's sovereignty does not make us puppets and God a villain, because God is good and therefore not guilty of sin. He is able to ordain even sinful actions while remaining holy, because he transcends this creation.

Charles Spurgeon, when asked how to harmonize human responsibility with God's sovereignty, reportedly said: "Friends, I do not try to harmonize them. These are two rails; two truths that the Bible teaches. Somewhere in eternity they meet." Sometimes we have to be satisfied with that, because we are finite and God is infinite.

Hannah found absolute comfort in God's sovereignty. It was not something she tried to run away from.

### 3. She Knew That God Treats His Saints Graciously (v. 9)

> *"He will guard the feet of His saints, but the wicked shall be silent in darkness."*

God acts according to his covenant grace toward those who are his. He guards them, keeps them, protects them.

### 4. She Knew That God Would Establish His Kingdom Through His Anointed King (v. 10)

> *"The Lord will judge the ends of the earth. He will give strength to His king, and exalt the horn of His anointed."*

Before the first king of Israel had even come, Hannah knew that the Lord's Anointed would be the ruler of the world. The word "anointed" in Hebrew is *Messiah* — in Greek, *Christ*. This is a remarkable prophecy from a woman in the era of the judges.

## Conclusion: Do You Know Your God?

Daniel 11:32 says: *"The people who know their God shall be strong and carry out great deeds."*

Where are you today? Do you know your God? Have you come to know Jesus Christ by faith — and having believed, have you grown in his grace and knowledge?

Hannah teaches us that we must know our God. To know our God rightly is eternal life. Knowing our God through Jesus Christ is our only hope in life and in death.

Do you know your God? If not, let Hannah teach you this great God whom we serve.
